title: Honey and walnut tart
subtitle: A rich, glossy nut tart with honey, butter and cream
excerpt: Walnuts in a filling of honey, cream, butter and eggs, set in a blind-baked sweet pastry case. The filling is cooked on the stovetop first to start the caramelisation before baking, which gives a deeper, more rounded flavour than mixing the ingredients cold. It cuts into clean slices and keeps for three days at room temperature. The total time is about 1 hour 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Nut tarts in the style of the American pecan pie have been adapted across British and European patisserie using local nuts. Walnut tarts with honey appear in French cooking from the Perigord and Dordogne, where both walnuts and honey are regional products. The method here draws on Richard Olney's 'Simple French Food' (1974) and on Mary Berry's 'Baking Bible' (1994) for the British baking context.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tart keeps at room temperature for 3 days, covered. The filling sets firmer on the second day and slices more cleanly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind-bake the case at 190 degrees C first

glossaryTerms.definition: The filling for a nut tart sets because the eggs in the mixture coagulate during baking, trapping the other ingredients in a firm but glossy layer. The filling should reach 80 to 85 degrees C at the centre to set fully without curdling. A slight wobble in the centre when the tart comes out of the oven is correct: the filling continues to set as it cools and will be firm and sliceable once cold. Overbaked filling turns grainy and separated.

content.text: The filling here is partially cooked on the stovetop before it goes into the oven. This pre-cooking starts the caramelisation of the honey and sugar and gives the finished tart a deeper flavour. Understanding
how a nut tart filling sets
is helpful here: remove the tart when the centre still wobbles slightly; it will firm up as it cools and slice cleanly once cold.

Toast the walnuts before adding them to the filling: 8 minutes at 180 degrees C until fragrant and lightly golden. Toasted walnuts are substantially less bitter than raw and the flavour holds better against the honey.

Rub the 100 g cold butter into the 200 g flour and 40 g icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Mix in the egg yolk and enough ice-cold water to bring together into a dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Roll the pastry and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Prick the base, line with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Blind-bake for 15 minutes, then remove the beans and paper and bake for a further 5 to 8 minutes until the base is pale golden. Reduce the oven to 175 degrees C.

In a saucepan, heat the 150 g honey, 50 g soft brown sugar, 100 ml double cream and 40 g butter together over medium heat, stirring, until the sugar has dissolved and the mixture is smooth. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the 2 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla. Scatter the 200 g chopped walnuts into the pastry case, then pour over the filling. Bake at 175 degrees C fan for 28 to 35 minutes until the filling is set at the edges and has a slight wobble only at the very centre, and the surface is deep amber brown. Cool completely before slicing.

items.problem: Filling is still liquid in the centre after 35 minutes
items.solution: Cover the tart loosely with foil to prevent the pastry edge from burning, and continue baking in 5-minute increments. The filling thickens further as it cools; a slight wobble is acceptable when it comes out of the oven.

The filling was overbaked or the eggs were added while the mixture was too hot. The butter-honey mixture must cool for at least 5 minutes before the eggs go in. If the mixture is still steaming, wait longer.

items.problem: Pastry case crumbled when removing from the tin
items.solution: The pastry was too short or the case was not fully cooked. Use a loose-bottomed tin rather than a solid one, and let the tart cool completely before removing the outer ring.
